description: This figure presents engineering stress-strain curves for Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) based austenitic low-density steels, specifically comparing three alloy compositions: 28Mn0Ni (Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.9C), 28Mn5Ni (Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.9C-5Ni), and 15Mn5Ni (Fe-15Mn-9Al-0.9C-5Ni). The curves depict the mechanical response across different processing conditions including hot-rolled and cold-rolled (50% reduction) states, as well as after heat treatment at various temperatures ranging from 873 K to 1273 K for 1 hour followed by water quenching. The as-cold-rolled samples exhibit high yield strengths (1350-1665 MPa) and ultimate tensile strengths (1500-1800 MPa) with limited elongation (5-11%). Upon heat treatment, the 28Mn0Ni alloy shows a progressive decrease in strength and remarkable increase in ductility, achieving 86% elongation at 1173 K with yield strength of 504 MPa. In contrast, the 28Mn5Ni alloy displays an anomalous peak strength at 973 K (YS 1450 MPa, UTS 1645 MPa) but with drastically reduced elongation (5%), attributed to concurrent precipitation of κ-carbides and B2 intermetallic phases. The 15Mn5Ni alloy exhibits the highest as-rolled strength (YS 1665 MPa) but shows brittle behavior at lower heat treatment temperatures due to high volume fractions of B2 stringers and κ-carbides at grain boundaries.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization of three Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) austenitic low-density steels—28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni, and 15Mn5Ni—in the hot-rolled plus cold-rolled (HR+CR) condition. The micrographs are arranged in a 3×3 grid: rows correspond to alloy compositions (28Mn0Ni: a–c; 28Mn5Ni: d–f; 15Mn5Ni: g–i), while columns display colored inverse pole figure (IPF) maps for austenite (left), image quality (IQ) maps (center), and phase contrast maps distinguishing FCC (red) and B2 (blue) phases (right). The 28Mn0Ni alloy (a–c) exhibits deformed austenitic grains with parallel planar slip bands indicated by the blue arrow in (b), and phase map (c) confirms a single FCC phase. The 28Mn5Ni alloy (d–f) shows similar deformed austenite microstructure but with sparse B2 precipitates visible as blue spots in (f). Notably, the 15Mn5Ni alloy (g–i) displays extensive blue B2 phase stringers aligned along the rolling direction within the red FCC matrix; the inset in (g) provides an IPF map of the B2 phase alone, where yellow arrows highlight shearing and fragmentation of these brittle B2 stringers during cold rolling. The scale bar represents 20 μm. These observations demonstrate that Ni addition promotes B2 intermetallic formation, particularly in the lower-Mn 15Mn5Ni composition, where the B2 phase undergoes mechanical fragmentation during cold deformation.

Figure 2 presents X-ray diffraction patterns comparing phase evolution in two Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) austenitic low-density steels: (a) 28Mn0Ni (Ni-free, high Mn) and (b) 15Mn5Ni (5% Ni, lower Mn). Each subplot displays three processing conditions: as cold-rolled (HR+CR), and after annealing at 973 K or 1173 K for 1 hour. The diffraction patterns identify γ-austenite (FCC, black squares) as the primary matrix phase across all conditions. After heat treatment at 973 K, both alloys exhibit additional peaks corresponding to κ-carbide (red circles) and B2 ordered phase (blue triangles), confirming precipitation of these secondary phases during intermediate-temperature annealing. The 15Mn5Ni alloy displays more intense B2 peaks at 973 K compared to 28Mn0Ni, indicating that Ni addition promotes B2 precipitation, particularly at lower Mn contents. At 1173 K, the 15Mn5Ni alloy retains distinct B2 phase reflections while 28Mn0Ni shows predominantly austenitic structure, demonstrating that alloy composition and processing temperature critically govern the competitive precipitation of κ-carbide and B2 intermetallic phases.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization results showing the microstructural evolution of three Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) austenitic low-density steels—28Mn0Ni (first row), 28Mn5Ni (second row), and 15Mn5Ni (third row)—after hot rolling, cold rolling (50% reduction), and subsequent heat treatment at 1023 K for 1 hour (HR+CR+1023 K condition). The nine sub-panels are organized in a 3×3 matrix: the first column [(a), (d), (g)] displays colored inverse pole figure (IPF) maps for the austenite phase revealing grain orientations with respect to the normal direction (ND), with an inset in (d) showing fine recrystallized grains at higher magnification; the second column [(b), (e), (h)] shows phase contrast maps where red indicates FCC (austenite) and blue indicates B2 ordered intermetallic phase; the third column [(c), (f), (i)] presents Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps with a color scale from Min 0° to Max 5°, indicating local strain distribution. The 28Mn0Ni alloy exhibits fully recrystallized equiaxed austenite grains with minimal B2 precipitation and low KAM values (deep blue), indicating complete strain relief. The 28Mn5Ni alloy shows partially recrystallized elongated grains with limited B2 phase at grain boundaries and moderate strain localization. In contrast, the 15Mn5Ni alloy displays a heavily banded microstructure with extensive B2 phase decoration along boundaries (prominent blue in h) and high local strain concentration (green/red in i), correlating with the reported brittle behavior due to intergranular cracking.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) orientation imaging microscopy data showing the microstructural evolution of Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) based austenitic low-density steels under different heat treatment conditions. The image displays colored inverse pole figure (IPF) maps for the austenite phase (γ-FCC) and phase distribution maps for three alloy compositions: 28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni, and 15Mn5Ni. Subfigures (a) and (b) show 28Mn0Ni, (c) and (d) show 28Mn5Ni, and (e) and (f) show 15Mn5Ni alloys processed by hot rolling plus cold rolling (HR+CR) followed by heat treatment at 973 K (left column) and 1173 K (right column), respectively. Subfigures (g) and (h) present phase maps of 15Mn5Ni alloy under the same conditions, with the legend indicating blue for B2 phase and red for FCC austenite. The IPF maps reveal that at 973 K, all alloys exhibit partially recrystallized microstructures with deformed grain remnants, while at 1173 K, significant grain growth occurs in the Ni-free 28Mn0Ni alloy, whereas the Ni-containing alloys (28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni) maintain finer grain structures due to the pinning effect of B2 intermetallic particles. The phase maps demonstrate the dissolution of finer B2 particles with increasing heat treatment temperature, with B2 stringers being more resistant to dissolution than fine particles. The scale bar indicates 20 μm, and the standard orientation triangle shows the crystallographic orientation color coding relative to the normal direction (ND). An inset in (f) (as noted in caption) shows the IPF map for B2 phase alone revealing extended recovery of B2 grains at 1173 K. These observations support that heat treatment in the two-phase (γ+B2) region effectively controls austenite grain growth in Ni-containing low-density steels.

This figure presents a four-panel quantitative analysis of microstructural evolution in Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) based austenitic low-density steels (28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni, and 15Mn5Ni) following cold rolling and subsequent heat treatment at temperatures ranging from 873 K to 1273 K. Panel (a) shows the recrystallization fraction of austenite as a function of heat treatment temperature, revealing that the Ni-free 28Mn0Ni alloy achieves nearly complete recrystallization (~0.9) at 1023 K, whereas the Ni-containing alloys (28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni) exhibit significantly lower recrystallization fractions (~0.4-0.5) even at 1173 K. Panel (b) displays the grain size of recrystallized austenite, demonstrating dramatic grain coarsening in 28Mn0Ni at 1173 K (~40 μm) compared to the stable fine grain structure (<5 μm) maintained in both Ni-containing alloys at the same temperature. Panel (c) illustrates the twin fraction evolution, where 28Mn0Ni shows increasing twin density with temperature, reaching ~0.55 at 1173 K, while 28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni exhibit lower twin fractions (~0.35 and ~0.2, respectively). Panel (d) tracks the dissolution behavior of the secondary B2 phase, showing that 15Mn5Ni initially contains the highest B2 volume fraction (~0.4 at 973 K), which decreases with increasing temperature, while 28Mn5Ni starts with lower B2 content (~0.11) and follows a similar dissolution trend. The horizontal dashed lines indicate the prior B2 phase volume fractions in the hot-rolled conditions. These data collectively demonstrate that Ni addition delays recrystallization and suppresses grain growth, while Mn content influences the stability of B2 intermetallic phases.

Figure 6 presents transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) based austenitic low-density steels after cold rolling and subsequent heat treatment at 973 K, specifically comparing 28Mn0Ni (a-d), 28Mn5Ni (e-f), and 15Mn5Ni (g-h) alloys. The set comprises bright-field (BF) images (a, c, e, g) and dark-field (DF) images (b, d, f, h) captured using the (010)κ satellite spot corresponding to κ-carbides, as indicated by selected area electron diffraction patterns (SAED/SADP) insets in (a-d) showing the [101] zone axis with marked 010κ and 101κ reflections. The images reveal the precipitation morphology and distribution of κ-carbides (marked by dotted orange arrows) and B2 intermetallic particles (intergranular indicated by broken yellow arrows and intragranular by solid white arrows). In the Ni-free 28Mn0Ni alloy, fine uniformly distributed intragranular κ-carbides are observed within recrystallized austenite regions (b), whereas deformed austenite regions contain inhomogeneous, relatively coarse κ-carbides of 10-60 nm (d). For Ni-containing alloys (28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni), both fine intragranular B2 particles (5-20 nm) and coarser intergranular B2 particles (50-200 nm) are visible alongside κ-carbides (e-h). The DF images specifically highlight κ-carbide distributions, showing higher density and coarser sizes in deformed austenite regions compared to recrystallized areas. These observations support that Ni addition promotes B2 precipitation and that highly strained regions serve as preferential nucleation sites for both κ-carbides and B2 particles, critically influencing the microstructural evolution and mechanical property balance in these low-density steels.

This figure presents six TEM bright-field micrographs captured at different locations showing the microstructural evolution of Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) based austenitic low-density steels after cold rolling and subsequent heat treatment at 1173 K. Subfigures (a) and (b) display the Ni-free 28Mn0Ni alloy, revealing austenite (γ) grains with no secondary phase precipitates present. Subfigures (c) and (d) show the 28Mn5Ni alloy, where both intragranular (yellow dashed arrows) and intergranular (white arrows) B2 particles are clearly visible within the austenite matrix. Subfigures (e) and (f) depict the 15Mn5Ni alloy, also exhibiting B2 precipitates distributed at grain boundaries and within grains. The comparison demonstrates that while the Ni-free alloy remains single-phase austenite at this temperature, Ni-containing steels exhibit B2 precipitation, with the fraction of intragranular B2 decreasing as the recrystallized austenite fraction increases. This indicates that highly strained regions serve as preferential nucleation sites for these precipitates, and caption states that B2 is an FeAl-based phase containing significant Mn and Ni.

This figure presents Vickers hardness (VHN) data for three Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) austenitic low-density steels—28Mn0Ni (Ni-free), 28Mn5Ni (high Mn with Ni), and 15Mn5Ni (lower Mn with Ni)—processed via cold rolling and subsequent heat treatment. Subfigure (a) plots hardness versus percentage reduction in thickness during cold rolling (0% to 50%). All alloys exhibit substantial work hardening: initial hot-rolled hardness values of ~250 VHN (28Mn0Ni), ~260 VHN (28Mn5Ni), and ~380 VHN (15Mn5Ni) converge to similar values of ~430–460 VHN after 50% reduction, indicating comparable work-hardening capacity despite different initial microstructures. Subfigure (b) shows hardness as a function of heat treatment temperature (873 K to 1273 K) for 50% cold-rolled samples annealed for 1 hour. Hardness decreases with increasing temperature for all alloys, but the softening behavior differs significantly. The Ni-free 28Mn0Ni alloy displays the steepest decline, dropping from ~430 VHN at 873 K to ~190 VHN at 1273 K. In contrast, 15Mn5Ni maintains the highest hardness across the temperature range (~500 VHN at 873 K to ~300 VHN at 1273 K), with 28Mn5Ni showing intermediate retention. These trends suggest that Ni addition and lower Mn content enhance high-temperature hardness stability, likely due to the presence of B2 intermetallic particles and κ-carbides that retard recrystallization and grain growth during annealing.

Figure 9 presents the tensile mechanical behavior of Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) austenitic low-density steels (28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni, and 15Mn5Ni) under different processing conditions. Panel (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves for the hot-rolled and cold-rolled (HR+CR) condition, where 15Mn5Ni exhibits the highest strength (~1800 MPa) but limited ductility. Panel (b) displays curves after heat treatment at various temperatures (973–1173 K), revealing significant variations in strength-ductility trade-offs. Panel (c) presents true stress-true strain curves for selected heat-treated conditions, while panel (d) shows ln(σ) versus ln(ε) plots used to determine strain-hardening exponents (n). The curves exhibit typical two-slope characteristics with n values increasing from 0.16 to 0.50 for 28Mn0Ni as heat treatment temperature rises from 973 K to 1173 K, whereas Ni-containing alloys (28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni) show lower n values (~0.30) at 1173 K. This indicates superior strain-hardening capability in Ni-free alloys compared to Ni-containing counterparts, correlating with microstructural evolution during deformation.

This figure presents fractographic and microstructural analysis of Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) low-density steels processed under HR+CR+1023 K condition. Subfigures (a)-(c) display SEM fractographs of failed tensile specimens for 28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni, and 15Mn5Ni alloys, respectively. Both 28Mn0Ni and 28Mn5Ni exhibit typical dimple morphology indicating ductile fracture nature, whereas 15Mn5Ni shows distinctive quasi-cleavage fracture features with numerous microcracks, suggesting crack initiation-controlled brittle failure. Subfigures (d)-(f) provide detailed cross-sectional analysis of the 15Mn5Ni alloy: (d) shows intergranular cracks in a low-magnification SEM image (200 μm scale), while (e) presents an OIM-IPF (Orientation Imaging Microscopy-Inverse Pole Figure) map revealing grain orientation distribution, and (f) displays a phase contrast map identifying FCC phase (red) and B2 phase (blue). The EBSD analysis confirms that crack propagation occurs preferentially along γ/B2 interphase boundaries, correlating with the negligible ductility (<1%) observed in 15Mn5Ni at this heat treatment temperature due to extensive secondary phase precipitation at grain boundaries forming precipitate-rich zones.

This figure presents a set of TEM bright-field images illustrating the post-tensile deformed microstructures of the 28Mn0Ni alloy (Fe-Mn-Al-C-based austenitic low-density steel without Ni addition) under different heat treatment conditions and strain levels. Subfigure (a) shows the HR+CR+973 K condition (partially recrystallized) deformed to a true strain of ε~0.14, exhibiting dislocation multiplication and cell formation resulting from fine cross-slip activity in deformed austenite regions. Subfigures (b)-(e) display the progressive substructure evolution in the HR+CR+1173 K condition (fully recrystallized) at increasing strain levels: (b) ε~0.02, (c) ε~0.08, and (d) ε~0.30, demonstrating refinement of planar slip bands from sparse dislocation arrays to dense, fine slip band structures with applied strain. Subfigure (e) specifically highlights the interaction between dislocations and prior annealing twins (indicated by white arrows). The images collectively reveal that the Ni-free alloy exhibits homogeneous slip band spacing (~200±30 nm) through dynamic refinement during deformation, contrasting with inhomogeneous slip behavior in Ni-containing variants. These observations support the conclusion that dislocation-twin interactions and dynamic slip band refinement serve as dominant strain hardening mechanisms in fully recrystallized austenite grains.

This figure presents four transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images showing post-tensile deformed microstructures of 15Mn5Ni alloy (Fe-15Mn-9Al-0.9C-5Ni, an austenitic low-density steel) subjected to HR+CR+1173 K heat treatment condition. The micrographs capture the evolution of dislocation structures at increasing true strain levels: (a) ε~0.02, (b) ε~0.05, (c) ε~0.14, and (d) continues the high magnification view at ε~0.14. Panel (a) specifically illustrates dislocation-B2 particle interaction, where dislocation lines are pinned by or bowing around B2 precipitates. As strain increases from (b) to (d), the images reveal progressive dislocation multiplication and the refinement of slip bands into finer, more densely packed configurations. All images share a 200 nm scale bar. The sequence demonstrates the strain-dependent transition from isolated dislocation-precipitate interactions to developed planar slip band structures, supporting the conclusion that dynamic slip band refinement and dislocation-precipitate interactions are key mechanisms governing the work hardening behavior in this alloy system.
